desmos.instructions: Calculate the new position

calculator.description: Verify the shift calculation

steps.title: State the final domain
steps.content:
The domain is the set of all real numbers except for the shifted restriction. We write this using set notation.

$$x \in \mathbb{R}, \quad x \neq 2$$

parts.content: Find the range of the function $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
- State that $y = 0$ is not in the range <marks>A1</marks>

- Express range correctly: $y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq 0$ or $(-\infty,0) \cup (0,\infty)$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Accept equivalent notation for expressing the range</note>

<commonMistake>Forgetting to exclude y = 0 from the range</commonMistake>

steps.title: Analyze the function structure
steps.content:
We need to find the **range** of the function, which represents all possible output values ($y$ or $f(x)$). 

The given function is a rational function with a constant numerator:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{x-2}$$

steps.title: Apply Asymptotic Analysis
steps.content:
Using **Asymptotic Analysis**, we examine if the function can ever equal zero. 

A fraction $\frac{a}{b}$ is equal to zero **only** if the numerator $a$ is zero (and the denominator is not).

In our function $f(x) = \frac{1}{x-2}$:
- The numerator is the constant **1**.
- Since $1 \neq 0$, the fraction can **never** equal zero.

This means $y = 0$ is a **horizontal asymptote** and is the only value excluded from the range.

steps.title: Verify with calculator
steps.content: We can confirm this by graphing the function. You will see that as $x$ gets very large (positive or negative), the curve gets closer and closer to the x-axis ($y=0$) but never actually touches or crosses it.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter y = 1 / (x - 2)
3. Draw the graph
4. Notice the curve approaches the x-axis but does not intersect it

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = 1 / (X - 2)
3. Press [GRAPH]
4. Observe the horizontal line y=0 is an asymptote

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f(x) = 1/(x-2)
2. Observe the horizontal asymptote at y = 0

calculator.description: Graph the function to visualize the horizontal asymptote at y=0

steps.title: State the range
steps.content:
Since $f(x)$ can take any real value except zero, we express the range by excluding 0 from the set of real numbers.

Range:
$$y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq 0$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To find the range using the algebraic rearrangement method, we start by setting $y$ equal to the function. This allows us to treat $y$ as the input and solve for $x$.

$$y = \frac{1}{x-2}$$

steps.title: Rearrange to solve for x
steps.content:
Now, we manipulate the equation to isolate $x$ in terms of $y$.

Multiply both sides by $(x-2)$:
$$y(x-2) = 1$$

Divide by $y$:
$$x-2 = \frac{1}{y}$$

Add 2 to both sides:
$$x = \frac{1}{y} + 2$$

steps.title: Identify restrictions on y
steps.content:
Examine the rearranged equation for any values of $y$ that would make the expression undefined.

$$x = \frac{1}{y} + 2$$

Since $y$ is in the denominator, we cannot divide by zero. Therefore, $y$ cannot be equal to 0. This means the horizontal asymptote is $y=0$.

highlights.text: State that y = 0 is not in the range

steps.title: Verify with graph
steps.content: We can verify this by graphing the function. You will see the curve approach the line $y=0$ (the x-axis) but never touch or cross it, confirming that $0$ is not in the range.

gdc.instructions: In Graph mode, enter y = 1/(x-2). Observe the horizontal asymptote.

desmos.instructions: Type y = 1/(x-2) into the input line.

calculator.description: Graph the function to identify the horizontal asymptote.

steps.title: State the range
steps.content:
The range includes all real numbers except for the restricted value. 

$$y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq 0$$

Alternatively, using interval notation:
$$(-\infty, 0) \cup (0, \infty)$$

steps.title: Visualize the function
steps.content:
To find the range using the **Graphical Approach**, we first sketch the graph of the function to see which $y$-values are produced. 

The function is a rational function:
$$f(x) = \frac{1}{x-2}$$

steps.title: Graph using GDC
steps.content: Use your graphing calculator to plot the function. Look specifically for the behavior as $x$ gets very large (positive and negative) to identify any horizontal boundaries.

2. Enter y1 = 1/(x-2)
3. Draw the graph using standard view (V-Window: STD)

2. Enter Y1 = 1/(X-2)
3. Press [ZOOM] > 6:ZStandard to view the graph

steps.title: Identify horizontal asymptote
steps.content:
Observe the graph. You will see a hyperbola with two branches:
- As $x \to \infty$, the graph gets closer and closer to the $x$-axis ($y=0$) from above.
- As $x \to -\infty$, the graph gets closer to the $x$-axis from below.

The graph approaches the line $$y = 0$$ but never touches or crosses it. This is the **horizontal asymptote**.

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
The **range** is the set of all possible $y$-values. Looking at the sketch:
- The graph extends infinitely upwards (positive $y$-values).
- The graph extends infinitely downwards (negative $y$-values).
- The only $y$-value that the function never takes is the value of the horizontal asymptote.

Therefore, the range is all real numbers except $0$.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
Write the range formally. You can use set notation or interval notation.

$$y \in \mathbb{R}, y \neq 0$$

Alternatively:
$$(-\infty, 0) \cup (0, \infty)$$

parts.content: Sketch the graph of the function $f(x)$.
parts.markscheme:
![Image](https://pub-images.revisiondojo.com/plots/0a9be56b-3a45-4297-be88-b5342a4bf05d.png)

- Identify vertical asymptote at $x = 2$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify horizontal asymptote at $y = 0$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify $y$-intercept at $(0,-\frac{1}{2})$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Sketch correct curve shape <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

<note>Graph should show:
- Hyperbolic shape
- No $x$-intercepts
- Approaches but never touches asymptotes
- Correct orientation in quadrants I and III (relative to asymptotes)</note>

<examTip>Draw light guidelines for the asymptotes first before sketching the curve</examTip>

steps.title: Find the vertical asymptote
steps.content:
Using the **Key Features Analysis** approach, we first determine the vertical asymptote by setting the denominator equal to zero. This represents the $x$-value where the function is undefined.

$$x - 2 = 0 \implies x = 2$$

The vertical asymptote is the line $x = 2$.

steps.title: Find the horizontal asymptote
steps.content:
Next, we find the horizontal asymptote by analyzing the end behavior of the function as $x$ approaches infinity. 

For the function $f(x) = \frac{1}{x-2}$, the degree of the numerator (which is 0) is less than the degree of the denominator (which is 1). Therefore, as $x$ becomes very large, the value of the function approaches zero.

$$\lim_{x \to \infty} \frac{1}{x-2} = 0$$

The horizontal asymptote is the line $y = 0$ (the $x$-axis).

steps.title: Calculate the y-intercept
steps.content:
To determine the $y$-intercept, we set $x = 0$ and evaluate the function.

$$f(0) = \frac{1}{0 - 2} = -\frac{1}{2}$$

The graph intersects the $y$-axis at the point $(0, -0.5)$.

This concept is covered in **Topic 2.8: Reciprocal and simple rational functions**.

gdc.instructions: Type 1/(0-2) and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Enter 1/(0-2) and press ENTER

desmos.instructions: Type 1/(0-2) in the input bar

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the y-intercept

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
Finally, we combine these features to sketch the graph:

1.  Draw dashed lines for the asymptotes at $x = 2$ and $y = 0$.
2.  Mark the $y$-intercept at $(0, -0.5)$.
3.  Sketch the hyperbolic curves approaching the asymptotes.
    - Since $f(3) = 1 > 0$, the curve is in the first quadrant relative to the asymptotes (top-right).
    - Since the $y$-intercept is negative, the other branch is in the third quadrant relative to the asymptotes (bottom-left).

Ensure the curve approaches but never touches the asymptotes.

calculator.description: Verify the graph shape

steps.title: Identify the parent function
steps.content:
Using the **Transformation Approach**, we start with the parent rational function:

$$y = \frac{1}{x}$$

This parent function has:
- A vertical asymptote at $x = 0$ (the y-axis)
- A horizontal asymptote at $y = 0$ (the x-axis)

steps.title: Apply horizontal translation
steps.content:
The function $f(x) = \frac{1}{x-2}$ replaces $x$ with $(x-2)$. In function transformations, $f(x-h)$ represents a horizontal translation of $h$ units to the **right**.

Therefore, we shift the entire graph of $y = \frac{1}{x}$ by 2 units to the right.

steps.title: Find vertical asymptote
steps.content:
Shifting the parent vertical asymptote ($x=0$) 2 units to the right gives the new vertical asymptote:

$$x = 2$$

steps.title: Find horizontal asymptote
steps.content:
A horizontal translation does not affect horizontal lines. Therefore, the horizontal asymptote remains the same as the parent function:

$$y = 0$$

steps.title: Calculate y-intercept
steps.content:
To find the $y$-intercept, set $x = 0$:

$$f(0) = \frac{1}{0-2} = -\frac{1}{2}$$

The graph intersects the y-axis at $(0, -0.5)$.

gdc.instructions:
1. In Run-Matrix mode, type 1 / ( 0 - 2 )
2. Press [EXE]

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [2nd] [MODE] (QUIT) to return to home screen
2. Type 1 / ( 0 - 2 ) and press [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type 1 / (0 - 2) in an empty cell

calculator.description: Calculate the y-intercept

steps.title: Sketch the graph
steps.content:
To complete the sketch:
1. Draw the vertical asymptote $x=2$ and horizontal asymptote $y=0$ as dashed lines.
2. Mark the $y$-intercept at $(0, -0.5)$.
3. Draw the two branches of the hyperbola:
   - Since the numerator is positive ($1 > 0$), the curve occupies the top-right and bottom-left quadrants relative to the asymptotes.
   - The right branch is in the region $x > 2, y > 0$.
   - The left branch is in the region $x < 2, y < 0$, passing through the y-intercept.

3. Press [DRAW]

Observe the graph on your screen. Notice the curve breaks into two parts and shoots towards positive or negative infinity at a specific $x$-value.

This vertical break happens where the denominator is zero:
$$x - 2 = 0 \implies x = 2$$

There is a **vertical asymptote** at $x = 2$. Draw this as a dashed vertical line.

steps.title: Identify the horizontal asymptote
steps.content:
Look at the far left and far right of the graph. As $x$ gets very large (positive or negative), the curve flattens out and gets closer and closer to the $x$-axis ($y=0$).

There is a **horizontal asymptote** at $y = 0$. Draw a dashed guideline along the $x$-axis.

steps.title: Sketch the final graph
steps.content:
Combine these features to draw the sketch:
1.  Draw a dashed vertical line at $x=2$.
2.  Plot the point $(0, -0.5)$.
3.  Draw the left branch in the third quadrant (relative to the asymptotes), passing through the $y$-intercept and approaching $y=0$ and $x=2$.
4.  Draw the right branch in the first quadrant (relative to the asymptotes), approaching $x=2$ from above and $y=0$ as $x \to \infty$.

Ensure the curves never touch the asymptotes.

parts.content: Use technology to solve the equation for $0 \le x \le 12$. Give all solutions correct to 3 d.p.
parts.markscheme:
- Graph or numerically solve $e^{0.25x} = \sin x + 1.6$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Solution: $x \approx 2.745$ <marks>A3</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Set up the graphing approach
steps.content: To solve the equation $$e^{0.25x} = \sin x + 1.6$$ using the **Intersection of Graphs** method, we will treat each side of the equation as a separate function. By plotting these two functions on the same set of axes, the $x$-coordinates of their intersection points will represent the solutions to the equation.

steps.title: Define the functions
steps.content:
We define two functions to enter into the graphing calculator:

$$\begin{aligned} y_1 &= e^{0.25x} \\ y_2 &= \sin x + 1.6 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Configure calculator settings
steps.content:
Before graphing, ensure your calculator is in **Radian** mode. This is crucial for all IB calculus and function questions involving trigonometry unless degrees are specifically stated.

Set your viewing window to match the domain specified in the question ($0 \le x \le 12$) to ensure you see all relevant solutions.

gdc.instructions:
1. Ensure angle setting is Radians.
2. In Graph menu, enter y1 = e^(0.25x) and y2 = sin(x) + 1.6.
3. Set V-Window x-min: 0, x-max: 12.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MODE] and select RADIAN.
2. Press [Y=].
3. Enter Y1 = e^(0.25X).
4. Enter Y2 = sin(X) + 1.6.
5. Press [WINDOW].
6. Set Xmin = 0, Xmax = 12.

desmos.instructions:
1. Click the wrench icon (Graph Settings) and ensure 'Radians' is selected.
2. Type y = e^{0.25x}.
3. Type y = \sin(x) + 1.6.
4. Set the x-axis range from 0 to 12.

calculator.description: Graph the functions and adjust the window

steps.title: Find the intersection point
steps.content:
Graph the functions and look for where they cross. You will observe that $y_1$ starts lower than $y_2$ but grows exponentially, while $y_2$ oscillates. They intersect exactly once within the domain.

Use the calculator's intersection tool to find the coordinates.

gdc.instructions:
1. Draw the graph.
2. Select G-Solve (F5).
3. Select ISCT (Intersection).
4. The calculator will display the x-coordinate.

2. Press [2nd] > [TRACE] (CALC).
3. Select 5: intersect.

calculator.description: Calculate the intersection point

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives the intersection at $x \approx 2.7452...$

The question asks for the solution correct to 3 decimal places.

$$x \approx 2.745$$

steps.title: Rearrange the equation
steps.content:
To use the **Finding Roots** (or Zeroes) approach, we first rearrange the given equation so that one side is equal to zero. This creates a single function $f(x)$ whose $x$-intercepts represent the solutions.

Subtract $(\sin x + 1.6)$ from both sides:
$$e^{0.25x} - \sin x - 1.6 = 0$$

Now, define the function:
$$f(x) = e^{0.25x} - \sin x - 1.6$$

$$x \approx 2.745$$

steps.content:

$$e^{0.25x} = \sin x + 1.6$$

The solver will converge to the intersection point.

parts.content: By considering the graphs, justify why no further solutions exist for $x > 12$.
parts.markscheme:
- Range of RHS: $\sin x + 1.6 \in [0.6, 2.6]$ <marks>M1</marks>
- Once $e^{0.25x} > 2.6$ (at $x > \frac{\ln 2.6}{0.25} \approx 3.822$), the exponential dominates; hence no intersections beyond this point (in particular none beyond 12) <marks>A1</marks>
- Exponential growth versus bounded sinusoid gives the conclusion <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the range of the RHS
steps.content:
First, let's determine the maximum possible value of the right-hand side of the equation. We know that the sine function oscillates between $-1$ and $1$.

Since $$-1 \le \sin x \le 1$$, we can add $1.6$ to the inequality:

$$0.6 \le \sin x + 1.6 \le 2.6$$

Therefore, the maximum value of the RHS is **2.6**.

steps.title: Calculate LHS at the boundary
steps.content:
Next, we calculate the value of the exponential function on the left-hand side at the specific boundary given, $$x = 12$$.

Substituting $$x = 12$$ into $$e^{0.25x}$$:

$$e^{0.25(12)} = e^3$$

gdc.instructions: Type e^3 and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [LN] (e^x), enter 3, then [ENTER]

desmos.instructions: Type exp(3) or e^3

calculator.description: Calculate e^3

steps.title: Compare values and conclude
steps.content:
Using the calculator value from the previous step:

$$e^3 \approx 20.1$$

Comparing the two sides at $$x=12$$:
- The exponential function is at approximately **20.1**
- The maximum possible value of the sine function is **2.6**

Since $$20.1 \gg 2.6$$, the exponential graph is already far above the sine graph at $$x=12$$. Because $$e^{0.25x}$$ is strictly increasing, it will continue to grow larger for all $$x > 12$$, while the RHS can never exceed 2.6. 

Therefore, the graphs cannot intersect again, and no further solutions exist.

steps.title: Analyze the RHS range
steps.content:
To show that no solutions exist for $x > 12$, we first determine the maximum possible value of the right-hand side (RHS) of the equation.

The sine function, $\sin x$, oscillates between $-1$ and $1$. Therefore, the range of the RHS is:

$$\begin{aligned} -1 &\le \sin x \le 1 \\ -1 + 1.6 &\le \sin x + 1.6 \le 1 + 1.6 \\ 0.6 &\le \sin x + 1.6 \le 2.6 \end{aligned}$$

The maximum value the RHS can ever reach is **2.6**.

steps.title: Set up divergence condition
steps.content:
Next, we find the specific $x$-value where the exponential function on the left-hand side (LHS), $e^{0.25x}$, exceeds this maximum bound of 2.6.

We set up the inequality to find where the LHS strictly dominates the RHS:

$$e^{0.25x} > 2.6$$

steps.title: Solve for the divergence point
steps.content:
We solve for $x$ by taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides using the log law $\log_a(a^x) = x$. Note that in the IB Data Booklet, natural log is denoted as $\ln x$.

$$\begin{aligned} \ln(e^{0.25x}) &> \ln(2.6) \\ 0.25x &> \ln(2.6) \\ x &> \frac{\ln(2.6)}{0.25} \\ x &> 4\ln(2.6) \end{aligned}$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate the value of 4 * ln(2.6)

ti84.instructions: Calculate the value of 4 * ln(2.6)

desmos.instructions: Calculate the value of 4 * ln(2.6)

calculator.description: Calculate the divergence point

steps.title: Calculate and conclude
steps.content:
Using a calculator, we find the value of the divergence point:

$$x \approx 3.82$$ 

This means that for all $x > 3.82$, the exponential term $e^{0.25x}$ is strictly greater than $2.6$, while the term $\sin x + 1.6$ is always less than or equal to $2.6$.

Since $12 > 3.82$, the exponential curve is already far above the sine curve at $x=12$. Therefore, no intersections (solutions) are possible for $x > 12$.

steps.title: Define the functions
steps.content:
To define the problem in terms of functions, let the left-hand side (LHS) be $f(x)$ and the right-hand side (RHS) be $g(x)$.

$$f(x) = e^{0.25x}$$

$$g(x) = \sin x + 1.6$$

We need to determine why these two functions do not intersect for $x > 12$.

steps.title: Compare function values at x = 12
steps.content:
First, let's establish the position of the curves at the start of the interval ($x=12$).

We calculate the value of the exponential function:
$$f(12) = e^{0.25(12)} = e^3 \approx 20.1$$

The sine function is bounded:
$$-1 \le \sin x \le 1$$
$$\Rightarrow 0.6 \le \sin x + 1.6 \le 2.6$$

At $x=12$, the exponential function ($20.1$) is already significantly higher than the maximum possible value of the sine function ($2.6$).

gdc.instructions: Calculate e^(3)

ti84.instructions: Calculate e^(0.25*12)

desmos.instructions: Type exp(3)

calculator.description: Calculate f(12) to compare with the maximum value of g(x)

steps.title: Calculate the derivatives
steps.content:
Next, we use the **Gradient/Growth Analysis** approach to compare how fast each function is changing. We find the derivative (slope) of each function using the chain rule and standard trigonometric derivatives.

For the LHS, using $\frac{d}{dx}(e^{kx}) = ke^{kx}$:
$$f'(x) = 0.25 e^{0.25x}$$

For the RHS, using $\frac{d}{dx}(\sin x) = \cos x$:
$$g'(x) = \cos x$$

steps.title: Compare growth rates for x > 12
steps.content:
Now we compare the magnitude of these slopes for $x > 12$.

The slope of the sine function is always bounded:
$$-1 \le g'(x) = \cos x \le 1$$

The slope of the exponential function at $x=12$ is:
$$f'(12) = 0.25 e^3 \approx 0.25(20.1) \approx 5.02$$

Since $f'(x)$ is an increasing function, for all $x > 12$:
$$f'(x) > 5.02$$

This means the exponential function is growing at a rate of at least $5$, while the sine function can never grow faster than a rate of $1$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate 0.25 * e^(3)

ti84.instructions: Calculate 0.25 * e^(3)

desmos.instructions: Calculate 0.25 * e^3

calculator.description: Calculate the slope of the exponential function at x=12

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content:
We have established two key facts for $x > 12$:
1. The exponential curve is already far above the sine curve ($20.1 > 2.6$).
2. The exponential curve is growing much faster ($slope > 5$) than the sine curve can possibly rise ($slope \le 1$).

Because the function that is "ahead" is also "running faster," the gap between them will strictly increase. Therefore, the curves can never intersect again for $x > 12$, and no further solutions exist.

parts.content: Consider instead $e^{0.25x} = \cos x + c$ with $c = 3.0$. State and justify the number of real solutions for $x \ge 0$.
parts.markscheme:
- Intersections require $e^{0.25x} \in [2, 4] \implies x \in \left[\frac{\ln 2}{0.25}, \frac{\ln 4}{0.25}\right] \approx [2.773, 5.545]$ <marks>M1</marks>
- On this interval (length $< \pi$), technology shows exactly one crossing <marks>M1</marks>
- Thus there is exactly one real solution <marks>A1</marks>
- Outside this $x$-band, values are incompatible, so no other solutions <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the range of the RHS
steps.content:
First, we determine the possible values for the right-hand side (RHS) of the equation. We know that the cosine function oscillates between $-1$ and $1$.

$$ -1 \le \cos x \le 1 $$

Adding the constant $c = 3.0$ to this inequality:

$$ -1 + 3 \le \cos x + 3 \le 1 + 3 $$
$$ 2 \le \cos x + 3 \le 4 $$

Therefore, the RHS is always in the interval $[2, 4]$.

steps.title: Set necessary condition for LHS
steps.content:
For a solution to exist, the left-hand side (LHS), $e^{0.25x}$, must lie within the same range as the RHS. If $e^{0.25x}$ is outside $[2, 4]$, it cannot equal $\cos x + 3$.

We set up the inequality:
$$ 2 \le e^{0.25x} \le 4 $$

steps.title: Solve for the interval of x
steps.content:
We solve this inequality to find the specific range of $x$ values where a solution could potentially exist.

Take the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of all parts:
$$ \ln 2 \le 0.25x \le \ln 4 $$

Divide by $0.25$ (which is multiplying by 4):
$$ 4\ln 2 \le x \le 4\ln 4 $$

Using the calculator to approximate these values:
$$ 2.77 \le x \le 5.55 $$

This means any real solution **must** lie within the interval $[2.77, 5.55]$.

gdc.instructions: Enter the expressions to find the decimal values.

ti84.instructions: Enter the expressions to find the decimal values.

desmos.instructions: Type the expressions in valid input boxes.

calculator.description: Calculate the boundary values for x

steps.title: Justify no solutions outside interval
steps.content:
We can now justify that there are no solutions outside this interval:

*   If $x < 2.77$, then $e^{0.25x} < 2$, but $\cos x + 3 \ge 2$. No intersection.
*   If $x > 5.55$, then $e^{0.25x} > 4$, but $\cos x + 3 \le 4$. No intersection.

Thus, we only need to search for solutions inside $[2.77, 5.55]$.

steps.title: Use technology to find solutions
steps.content:
Now we use a graphing calculator to visualize the functions $Y_1 = e^{0.25x}$ and $Y_2 = \cos x + 3$ specifically on the interval $x \in [2.77, 5.55]$.

*   At the lower bound ($x \approx 2.77$): $e^{0.25x} = 2$, while $\cos(2.77) + 3 \approx 2.07$. The curve $Y_2$ starts above $Y_1$.
*   At the upper bound ($x \approx 5.55$): $e^{0.25x} = 4$, while $\cos(5.55) + 3 \approx 3.74$. The curve $Y_1$ ends above $Y_2$.

Since the functions are continuous and swap positions (one starts lower and ends higher), they must intersect.

3. Set window settings to match the interval approx [2, 6]
4. Look for intersection points.

calculator.description: Graph the functions to count intersections

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
The graph shows exactly one point of intersection within the valid interval. Since we have proven no solutions exist outside this interval, there is exactly one solution in total.

**Answer:** There is exactly **1** real solution.

steps.title: Define the functions
steps.content:
To solve the equation $e^{0.25x} = \cos x + 3$ using a graphical approach, we define two functions to plot on our graphing calculator:

$$\begin{aligned} y_1 &= e^{0.25x} \\ y_2 &= \cos x + 3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Analyze the range of values
steps.content:
Before plotting, we can narrow down where solutions might exist by looking at the range of the cosine function.

Since $-1 \le \text{cos } x \le 1$, the function $y_2$ is bounded:
$$2 \le \text{cos } x + 3 \le 4$$

Solutions can only exist where $y_1$ is also between 2 and 4. Solving for the $x$ values corresponding to this range:

$$e^{0.25x} = 2 \Rightarrow 0.25x = \text{ln } 2 \Rightarrow x = 4\text{ln } 2 \approx 2.77$$
$$e^{0.25x} = 4 \Rightarrow 0.25x = \text{ln } 4 \Rightarrow x = 4\text{ln } 4 \approx 5.55$$

This means any possible solutions must lie in the interval $[2.77, 5.55]$.

gdc.instructions: Calculate the lower and upper bounds for x

ti84.instructions: Calculate the lower and upper bounds for x

desmos.instructions: Calculate the lower and upper bounds for x

calculator.description: Calculate the boundary values

steps.title: Plot the functions
steps.content: Now, use your graphing calculator to plot both functions. We are looking for intersection points within the identified window $x \approx 2.77$ to $5.55$.

gdc.instructions: In Graph mode, enter \y1 = e^(0.25x)\ and \y2 = cos(x) + 3\. Set View Window to x: [0, 7], y: [0, 5]. Draw.

ti84.instructions: Press Y=, enter \Y1 = e^(0.25x)\ and \Y2 = cos(x) + 3\. Press WINDOW and set Xmin=0, Xmax=7, Ymin=0, Ymax=5. Press GRAPH.

desmos.instructions: Type \y = e^{0.25x}\ and \y = \cos(x) + 3\. Adjust the view to see the intersection.

calculator.description: Plot the functions to visualize intersections

steps.title: Identify the intersection
steps.content:
Observe the graph in the interval $[2.77, 5.55]$.

- At the start ($x \approx 2.77$), the exponential curve $y_1$ is at 2, while the cosine wave $y_2$ is near its minimum (since $\cos(2.77)$ is negative), so $y_1 < y_2$.
- As $x$ increases, $y_1$ rises steadily.
- Near the end ($x \approx 5.55$), $y_1$ reaches 4. The cosine wave oscillates but stays below or equal to 4. Specifically, at $x \approx 5.55$, the exponential curve is significantly higher than the cosine wave.

The graph shows clearly that the curves cross exactly **once** in this interval.

gdc.instructions: Press G-Solv (F5) > ISCT (F5).

ti84.instructions: Press 2nd > TRACE (CALC), select 5:intersect. Move cursor to the intersection point and press ENTER three times.

desmos.instructions: Click on the point where the two graphs cross to see the coordinates.

calculator.description: Find the intersection point

steps.title: Confirm uniqueness outside the interval
steps.content:
To fully justify that there is only one solution for all $x \ge 0$, we consider the regions outside our interval:

*   **For $x < 2.77$**: $e^{0.25x} < 2$, but the minimum value of $\text{cos } x + 3$ is 2. The curves cannot intersect here (except potentially touching if bounds aligned perfectly, but the graph shows separation).
*   **For $x > 5.55$**: $e^{0.25x} > 4$, but the maximum value of $\text{cos } x + 3$ is 4. The exponential curve stays permanently above the cosine wave.

Therefore, no other solutions exist outside the interval.

highlights.text: Outside this x-band, values are incompatible

steps.title: State the final conclusion
steps.content:
Based on the graphical evidence of a single crossing within the valid range $[2.77, 5.55]$ and the incompatibility of values outside this range, there is exactly one real solution.

**Answer:** There is exactly **1** real solution.

steps.title: Identify the method
steps.content: To solve the equation $e^{0.15x} = \sin x + 2.5$ using the **Intersection of Graphs** method, we treat each side of the equation as a separate function. We are looking for the $x$-values where the graphs of these two functions cross each other.

steps.title: Define functions and window
steps.content:
Enter the left side of the equation as your first function ($y_1$) and the right side as your second function ($y_2$) into your graphing calculator.

1. First intersection: $x \approx 3.917$
2. Second intersection: $x \approx 6.392$
3. Third intersection: $x \approx 8.223$

This method directly visualizes that there are exactly three solutions in the given interval.

steps.title: Rearrange the equation
steps.content:
To use the **Finding Zeros** (or Roots) method, we first need to rearrange the equation so that one side equals zero. 

Subtract $(\sin x + 2.5)$ from both sides:

$$e^{0.15x} - \sin x - 2.5 = 0$$

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
To use a numerical equation solver, we first arrange the equation so that one side is equal to zero. 

Starting with:

$$e^{0.15x} = \sin x + 2.5$$

Subtract $(\sin x + 2.5)$ from both sides:

$$e^{0.15x} - \sin x - 2.5 = 0$$

This form $0 = f(x)$ allows the calculator to search for the roots (values of $x$) where the expression equals zero.

steps.content:
The numerical solver finds one solution at a time based on a starting guess. Since the domain is $0 \le x \le 12$, we should test guesses across this range to find all solutions.

Let's start with a guess in the lower part of the domain, such as $x = 3$.

To check for more solutions, we update our guess to a value further along the number line. Let's try a guess near the middle of the domain, such as $x = 6$.

The solver locates a second unique value.

steps.content:
We have found three solutions. Testing guesses near the boundaries ($x=0$ or $x=12$) does not yield new solutions within the domain. Rounding each value to 3 decimal places gives:

$$x \approx 3.917, \quad x \approx 6.392, \quad x \approx 8.223$$

parts.content: By considering the graphs, justify why no further solutions exist for \(x>12\).
parts.markscheme:
- State the range of the RHS: \(\sin x+2.5 \in [1.5,\, 3.5]\) <marks>M1</marks>

- Identify threshold where exponential exceeds maximum of sinusoid: \(x > \frac{\ln 3.5}{0.15} \approx 8.352\) <marks>A1</marks>

- Explain that for \(x > 8.352\), \(e^{0.15x} > 3.5\) permanently (exponential is unbounded; sinusoid is bounded), so no intersection beyond 12 <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the range of the RHS
steps.content:
First, we determine the maximum possible value of the right-hand side (RHS) function, $y = \sin x + 2.5$.

Since the maximum value of $\sin x$ is $1$, the maximum value of the entire expression is:
$$y_{\text{max}} = 1 + 2.5 = 3.5$$

This means the graph of the RHS will never go above the horizontal line $y = 3.5$.

steps.title: Set up the threshold equation
steps.content:
Now, we find the exact $x$-value where the exponential function on the left-hand side (LHS), $y = e^{0.15x}$, crosses this maximum threshold of $3.5$.

We solve the equation:

$$e^{0.15x} = 3.5$$

Taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides:

$$\begin{aligned} 0.15x &= \ln(3.5) \\ x &= \frac{\ln(3.5)}{0.15} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the threshold value
steps.content:
Using a calculator, we evaluate this critical $x$-value:

$$x = \frac{\ln(3.5)}{0.15} \approx 8.35$$

This tells us that at $x \approx 8.35$, the exponential function reaches a height of $3.5$. For any $x$ value larger than this, the exponential function will be strictly greater than $3.5$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Select Run-Matrix mode
2. Type ln(3.5) ÷ 0.15
3. Press EXE

desmos.instructions: Type ln(3.5)/0.15 in an input box

calculator.description: Calculate the critical x-value where the exponential equals 3.5

steps.title: Conclude for x > 12
steps.content:
Since $e^{0.15x}$ is an increasing function, for all $x > 8.35$, the value of the exponential is permanently greater than $3.5$.

$$e^{0.15x} > 3.5 \quad \text{for} \quad x > 8.35$$

Because $12 > 8.35$, the exponential function has already exceeded the maximum possible value of the sinusoidal function (3.5) long before $x=12$. Therefore, there can be no intersections for $x > 12$.

steps.title: Find the maximum value of the RHS
steps.content:
First, we determine the maximum possible value of the right-hand side function. We know that the sine function oscillates between $-1$ and $1$.

$$ -1 \le \sin x \le 1 $$

Adding 2.5 to the inequality gives the range of the RHS:

$$ 1.5 \le \sin x + 2.5 \le 3.5 $$

The maximum value the RHS can ever reach is $3.5$.

steps.title: Evaluate the LHS at x = 12
steps.content: Next, we evaluate the left-hand side function $e^{0.15x}$ at the boundary $x=12$ to see where the exponential curve is located relative to the sine wave's maximum.

gdc.instructions: Type e^(0.15*12) and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Press [2nd] [ln] (e^x), type 0.15 * 12, then [ENTER]

calculator.description: Calculate the value of the exponential function at x = 12

steps.title: Compare the values
steps.content:
Using the calculator, we find:

$$e^{0.15(12)} = e^{1.8} \approx 6.05$$

Comparing this to the maximum RHS value from Step 0:

$$6.05 > 3.5$$

At $x=12$, the exponential function is already strictly greater than the highest possible peak of the sine function.

steps.title: Justify no further solutions
steps.content:
The exponential function $f(x) = e^{0.15x}$ is strictly increasing for all real $x$. Since it is already above the maximum value of the RHS at $x=12$ (i.e., $6.05 > 3.5$), and it will only continue to grow larger, it can never intersect the bounded function $\sin x + 2.5$ again.

Therefore, there are no solutions for $x > 12$.

highlights.text: justify why no further solutions exist for \(x>12\)

parts.content: Consider instead \(e^{0.15x}=\cos x+c\) with \(c=5.0\). State and justify the number of real solutions for \(x\ge 0\).
parts.markscheme:
- Identify condition for intersection: \(e^{0.15x} \in [4, 6] \Rightarrow x \in \left[\frac{\ln 4}{0.15},\ \frac{\ln 6}{0.15}\right] \approx [9.242,\, 11.945]\) <marks>M1</marks>

- Observation that interval width (\(\approx 2.7\)) is less than \(\pi\), so exactly one crossing is possible/technology confirms one solution <marks>M1</marks>

- Conclude exactly one real solution <marks>A1</marks>

- Justify no other solutions exist outside this range <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Analyze the range of the right-hand side
steps.content:
First, we determine the possible values for the right-hand side (RHS) of the equation $e^{0.15x} = \cos x + 5.0$.

The cosine function, $\cos x$, oscillates between $-1$ and $1$. Therefore, the range of the RHS is:

$$[-1 + 5.0, \ 1 + 5.0] = [4, 6]$$

This means that for a solution to exist, the left-hand side (LHS), $e^{0.15x}$, must also lie within this interval:

$$4 \le e^{0.15x} \le 6$$

steps.title: Solve for the possible x-interval
steps.content:
Now, we solve the inequality for $x$ to find the restricted domain where solutions can occur. Taking the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of the inequality:

$$\ln(4) \le 0.15x \le \ln(6)$$

Dividing by 0.15:

$$x \in \left[\frac{\ln 4}{0.15}, \frac{\ln 6}{0.15}\right]$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate ln(4)/0.15 and ln(6)/0.15

ti84.instructions: Calculate ln(4)/0.15 and ln(6)/0.15 on the home screen

desmos.instructions: Type ln(4)/0.15 and ln(6)/0.15 in separate lines

calculator.description: Calculate the boundary values for the x-interval.

steps.title: Calculate interval width
steps.content:
Calculating the numerical values for the boundaries:

$$\begin{aligned} x_{min} &= \frac{\ln 4}{0.15} \approx 9.242 \\ x_{max} &= \frac{\ln 6}{0.15} \approx 11.945 \end{aligned}$$

The width of this interval is:

$$11.945 - 9.242 = 2.703$$

steps.title: Compare interval width to period
steps.content:
We now compare the interval width to the period of the cosine function. The period of $\cos x$ is $2\pi \approx 6.28$. A half-period (distance from a peak to a trough) is $\pi \approx 3.14$.

Since our interval width ($2.703$) is less than $\pi$, the cosine function cannot complete a full oscillation (e.g., go down and back up fully) within this range. It is largely monotonic or has at most one turning point.

Combined with the fact that $e^{0.15x}$ is strictly increasing, this suggests there cannot be multiple intersections.

steps.title: Verify boundary conditions
steps.content:
To confirm there is exactly one solution, we check the values at the boundaries of the interval $[9.242, 11.945]$:

**At the lower bound ($x \approx 9.242$):**
- LHS: $e^{0.15(9.242)} = 4$
- RHS: $\cos(9.242) + 5 \approx 4.02$
- Result: LHS < RHS

**At the upper bound ($x \approx 11.945$):**
- LHS: $e^{0.15(11.945)} = 6$
- RHS: $\cos(11.945) + 5 \approx 5.81$
- Result: LHS > RHS

Since the continuous LHS function starts below the RHS and ends above the RHS within this short interval, they must cross exactly once.

gdc.instructions:
1. Graph menu
2. y1 = e^(0.15x)
3. y2 = cos(x) + 5
4. Set View Window: x:[9, 12], y:[3, 7]
5. Draw and observe intersection

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Y1 = e^(0.15X)
3. Y2 = cos(X) + 5
4. Press [WINDOW] and set Xmin=9, Xmax=12, Ymin=3, Ymax=7
5. Press [GRAPH] to see the single intersection

desmos.instructions: Graph y = e^{0.15x} and y = cos(x) + 5. Zoom into the intersection around x = 10.6

calculator.description: Visualize the intersection to confirm exactly one solution.

steps.title: Conclusion
steps.content: Because the interval width is narrow ($$< \pi$$) and the functions cross from LHS < RHS to LHS > RHS, there is exactly **one real solution**.

steps.title: Define the functions
steps.content:
To solve the equation using a graphing calculator, we first define the two sides of the equation as separate functions. 

Enter the following into your GDC:

$$\begin{aligned} y_1 &= e^{0.15x} \\ y_2 &= \cos x + 5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Determine the search window
steps.content:
Before graphing, analyze the range of values to set an appropriate window. 

The function $y_2 = \cos x + 5$ oscillates between a minimum of $5-1=4$ and a maximum of $5+1=6$. 

The exponential function $y_1 = e^{0.15x}$ is strictly increasing. It will only intersect $y_2$ when its value is between 4 and 6. We can calculate the $x$-values for this range:

$$\begin{aligned} e^{0.15x} &= 4 \Rightarrow x = \frac{\ln 4}{0.15} \approx 9.24 \\ e^{0.15x} &= 6 \Rightarrow x = \frac{\ln 6}{0.15} \approx 11.95 \end{aligned}$$

Set your window x-min to 0 and x-max to 15 to clearly see this region.

steps.title: Identify number of solutions
steps.content:
Observe the graph on your calculator. 

You will see the exponential curve $y_1$ rising and cutting through the oscillating wave $y_2$. Specifically, $y_1$ enters the region $y \in [4, 6]$ at $x \approx 9.24$ and exits at $x \approx 11.95$.

Use the intersection tool to find the solution.

Select G-Solve (or Analysis).
Select Intersect.

Select 5: intersect.
Select First curve, Second curve, Guess.

calculator.description: Find Intersection

steps.title: Justify unique solution
steps.content:
The calculator shows exactly **one** intersection point.

To justify this analytically as required for full marks:
1. The width of the interval where intersection is possible is $11.95 - 9.24 \approx 2.71$.
2. The period of $\cos x$ is $2\pi \approx 6.28$, and the distance between a peak and a trough is $\pi \approx 3.14$.
3. Since the interval width ($2.71$) is less than $\pi$, the cosine function cannot complete enough of an oscillation to cross the strictly increasing exponential function more than once within this window.

Therefore, there is exactly one real solution.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content: There is **one** real solution.

questions.specification:
Let
\[
f(x)=e^{kx},\quad g(x)=\ln(x+4),\quad x>-4,\ k>0.
\]
It is given that \((f\circ g)(6)=5\).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > SL 2.5—Composite functions, identity, finding inverse > Question Type 2: Finding composite functions of two more complex functions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Finding composite functions of two more complex functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (c) and (e) require forming composite functions f∘g and g∘f and finding an inverse of a nontrivial composite/exponential-log function.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > SL 2.2—Functions, notation domain, range and inverse as reflection > Question Type 4: Finding the domain of more complex functions
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Finding the domain of more complex functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) asks for maximal domains of f∘g and g∘f, involving log/exponential domain restrictions.

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Functions > SL 2.10—Solving equations graphically and analytically > Question Type 2: Solving for x more difficult equations
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 2: Solving for x more difficult equations
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (d) asks to solve a transcendental equation (x+4)^k = ln(4+e^{kx}) numerically on x≥0 and justify uniqueness (root-finding/analysis of solutions).

labeledSubSubTopics.path: Number and Algebra > SL 1.7—Laws of exponents and logs > Question Type 4: Using laws of logarithms
labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 4: Using laws of logarithms
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Parts (b) and (d) involve manipulating equations with exponentials and logarithms (e.g., using ln and exp properties to isolate k / compare expressions).

parts.content: Determine \(k\) correct to 3 significant figures.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute \(x=6\) into \((f\circ g)(x)\): \((6+4)^k=10^k=5\) <marks>M1</marks>
- Solve for \(k\): \(k=\dfrac{\ln5}{\ln10}\) <marks>A1</marks>
- State result: \(k\approx 0.699\) <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

steps.title: Identify the composite function
steps.content:
The notation $(f \circ g)(6)$ represents the composite function $f(g(6))$. This means we must first evaluate the inner function $g$ at $x=6$, and then use that result as the input for the outer function $f$.

We are given the condition:
$$(f\circ g)(6)=5$$

steps.title: Evaluate the inner function
steps.content:
First, evaluate the inner function $g(x) = \ln(x+4)$ at $x=6$:

$$g(6) = \ln(6+4) = \ln(10)$$

steps.title: Substitute into outer function
steps.content:
Next, substitute the result $g(6) = \ln(10)$ into the outer function $f(x) = e^{kx}$.

Since the input $x$ in $f(x)$ is now $\ln(10)$, we replace $x$ with $\ln(10)$ in the exponent:

$$f(g(6)) = e^{k \ln(10)}$$

steps.title: Solve for k
steps.content:
Set the resulting expression equal to 5 as given in the question:

$$e^{k \ln(10)} = 5$$

To solve for $k$, take the natural logarithm ($\ln$) of both sides. This allows us to work with the exponent directly:

$$\begin{aligned} \ln\left(e^{k \ln(10)}\right) &= \ln(5) \\ k \ln(10) &= \ln(5) \\ k &= \frac{\ln(5)}{\ln(10)} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate the value
steps.content: Use a calculator to find the numerical value of $k$.

gdc.instructions: Type ln(5)/ln(10) and press EXE

desmos.instructions: Type ln(5)/ln(10)

calculator.description: Calculate the quotient of the natural logarithms

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives $k \approx 0.69897...$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:

$$k \approx 0.699$$

steps.title: Form the composite function
steps.content:
The composite function $(f \circ g)(x)$ is found by substituting the inner function $g(x)$ into the outer function $f(x)$.

$$f(g(x)) = f(\ln(x+4)) = e^{k \ln(x+4)}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
We can simplify this expression using the laws of logarithms and exponents. First, apply the power rule $a \ln b = \ln(b^a)$, and then use the inverse property $e^{\ln u} = u$.

$$\begin{aligned} f(g(x)) &= e^{\ln((x+4)^k)} \\ &= (x+4)^k \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute given values
steps.content:
The question states that when $x=6$, the value of the composite function is 5. Substitute these values into our simplified expression:

$$\begin{aligned} (6+4)^k &= 5 \\ 10^k &= 5 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Solve for k
steps.content:
To solve for the exponent $k$, we can take the logarithm of both sides. Using the base-10 logarithm is most direct here:

$$k = \log_{10}(5)$$

steps.title: Calculate and round
steps.content:
Using a calculator, find the value of $k$ and round it to 3 significant figures as required.

$$k \approx 0.69897...$$

$$k = 0.699 \quad (3 \text{ s.f.})$$

gdc.instructions: Press log, enter 5, then press EXE.

ti84.instructions: Press LOG, enter 5, then close parenthesis and press ENTER.

desmos.instructions: Type log(5) or log_10(5).

calculator.description: Calculate the logarithm to find k

steps.title: Form the composite function
steps.content:
First, we need to understand what the notation $(f \circ g)(6)$ means. It represents the composite function evaluated at $x = 6$, which is $f(g(6))$.

Let's calculate $g(6)$ first by substituting $x=6$ into the function $g(x)$:

$$g(6) = \ln(6 + 4) = \ln(10)$$

steps.title: Substitute into f(x)
steps.content:
Now substitute this result into $f(x)$. Since $f(x) = e^{kx}$, replacing $x$ with $\ln(10)$ gives:

$$f(g(6)) = e^{k \ln(10)}$$

The question tells us this value equals 5.

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
Equating our expression to the given value, we get the equation to solve:

$$e^{k \ln(10)} = 5$$

steps.title: Solve using GDC
steps.content: Since we are using the **GDC Numerical Solver** method, we will input this equation directly into the calculator to find $k$. There is no need to simplify algebraically first.

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Equation mode > Solver.
2. Type the equation: e^(x × ln(10)) = 5
3. Press [EXE] or [SOLVE].

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [MATH] > scroll down to 'Numeric Solver' (or 'Solver').
2. Enter E1: e^(X * ln(10))
3. Enter E2: 5
4. Press [OK] or [Graph]
5. Move cursor near a guess (e.g., 1) and press [ALPHA] [ENTER] (SOLVE).

calculator.description: Solve e^(x * ln(10)) = 5 for x (which represents k)

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The calculator gives the value $k \approx 0.69897...$

Rounding to 3 significant figures:

gdc.instructions: Check value manually: ln(5)/ln(10)

ti84.instructions: Check value manually: ln(5)/ln(10)

desmos.instructions: Check value manually: \frac{\ln(5)}{\ln(10)}

calculator.description: Verify the calculation: ln(5)/ln(10)

steps.title: Final Result
steps.content: $$k \approx 0.699$$

parts.content: Find \((f\circ g)(x)\), \((g\circ f)(x)\) and their maximal domains.
parts.markscheme:
- \((f\circ g)(x)=(x+4)^k\) for \(x>-4\) <marks>M1 A1</marks>
- \((g\circ f)(x)=\ln(e^{kx}+4)\) for all real \(x\) <marks>M1 A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Set up the first composite function
steps.content:
To find $(f\circ g)(x)$, we substitute the inner function $g(x)$ into the outer function $f(x)$.

Given:
$$f(x)=e^{kx}, \quad g(x)=\ln(x+4)$$

Substitute $g(x)$ into $f(x)$:
$$(f\circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = e^{k \cdot g(x)} = e^{k \ln(x+4)}$$

steps.title: Simplify using laws of logarithms
steps.content:
We simplify the expression using the power law for logarithms, $n \ln a = \ln(a^n)$, followed by the inverse property $e^{\ln a} = a$.

Applying the power law to the exponent:
$$k \ln(x+4) = \ln((x+4)^k)$$

Now substitute this back:
$$(f\circ g)(x) = e^{\ln((x+4)^k)}$$

Using the inverse property:
$$(f\circ g)(x) = (x+4)^k$$

gdc.instructions: Compare the original composite expression with the simplified form.

ti84.instructions: Calculate the composite function value directly and using the simplified formula.

desmos.instructions: Type the expression to evaluate.

calculator.description: Verify the simplification numerically with specific values (e.g., k=2, x=6).

steps.title: Determine the domain of (f ∘ g)(x)
steps.content:
The domain of a composite function requires the inner function to be defined. 

For $g(x) = \ln(x+4)$ to be defined, the argument must be positive:
$$x+4 > 0 \Rightarrow x > -4$$

The outer function $f(u) = e^{ku}$ accepts all real numbers, so there are no additional restrictions.

**Domain:** $x > -4$

gdc.instructions: Graph y = ln(x+4) and observe the vertical asymptote.

ti84.instructions: Press Y=, enter ln(x+4), and press GRAPH. Notice the asymptote at x = -4.

desmos.instructions: Graph the function to see the domain constraint.

calculator.description: Graph the inner function to visualize its domain.

steps.title: Set up the second composite function
steps.content:
To find $(g\circ f)(x)$, we substitute $f(x)$ into $g(x)$.

Substitute $f(x)=e^{kx}$ into $g(x)=\ln(x+4)$:
$$(g\circ f)(x) = g(f(x)) = \ln(f(x)+4)$$

Substituting the expression for $f(x)$:
$$(g\circ f)(x) = \ln(e^{kx} + 4)$$

steps.title: Determine the domain of (g ∘ f)(x)
steps.content:
The domain is determined by ensuring the inner function is defined and the outer function's argument is valid.

1. The inner function $f(x) = e^{kx}$ is defined for all real numbers ($x \in \mathbb{R}$).
2. The outer function $\ln(u)$ requires its argument $u$ to be positive. Here, $u = e^{kx} + 4$.

Since $e^{kx} > 0$ for all real $x$, it follows that $e^{kx} + 4 > 4$, which is always positive.

Therefore, the composite function is defined for all real numbers.

**Domain:** $x \in \mathbb{R}$

steps.title: Summary of results
steps.content:
We have found the composite functions and their maximal domains:

$$(f\circ g)(x) = (x+4)^k, \quad \text{Domain: } x > -4$$

$$(g\circ f)(x) = \ln(e^{kx}+4), \quad \text{Domain: } x \in \mathbb{R}$$

parts.content: Solve \((x+4)^k=\ln(4+e^{kx})\) on \(x\ge0\) (3 d.p.) and justify uniqueness.
parts.markscheme:
- Consider \(F(x)=(x+4)^k-\ln(4+e^{kx})\) <marks>M1</marks>
- \(F(0)=4^k-\ln5\approx 1.03 > 0\) and \(F(x)\to-\infty\) as \(x\to\infty\) <marks>M1 R1</marks>
- Numerical solution: \(x\approx 8.203\) <marks>M1 A1</marks>
- Exactly one solution by the same monotonicity argument for \(F'\) <marks>R1 A1</marks>

7 marks total

steps.title: Calculate the constant k
steps.content:
First, we use the given condition $(f \circ g)(6) = 5$ to find the value of $k$. 

We start by evaluating the inner function $g(x)$ at $x=6$:
$$\begin{aligned} g(6) &= \ln(6+4) \\ &= \ln(10) \end{aligned}$$

Now substitute this into $f(x)$:
$$\begin{aligned} (f \circ g)(6) &= f(\ln 10) \\ &= e^{k \ln 10} \\ &= (e^{\ln 10})^k \\ &= 10^k \end{aligned}$$

Set this equal to 5 and solve for $k$:
$$\begin{aligned} 10^k &= 5 \\ k &= \log_{10}(5) \end{aligned}$$

Using a calculator, $k \approx 0.69897$.

gdc.instructions: Use log10(5) or solve(10^k=5, k).

ti84.instructions: Press [log], enter 5, then [enter].

desmos.instructions: Type log_10(5).

calculator.description: Calculate the value of k

steps.title: Define the Difference Function F(x)
steps.content:
To solve $(x+4)^k = \ln(4+e^{kx})$, we move all terms to one side to define a difference function $F(x)$. We are looking for the root where $F(x)=0$.

$$F(x) = (x+4)^k - \ln(4+e^{kx})$$

We need to solve $F(x) = 0$ for $x \ge 0$.

steps.title: Solve F(x) = 0 using a GDC
steps.content:
We use a graphing calculator to find the root of the equation. Graph $y = (x+4)^{\log_{10}5} - \ln(4+e^{x \log_{10}5})$ and find the x-intercept.

The calculator gives a solution of approximately $x \approx 8.203$.

gdc.instructions:
1. Open Graph application.
2. Enter f1(x) = (x+4)^log10(5) - ln(4+e^(x*log10(5))).
3. Analyze Graph > Zero.

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=].
2. Enter Y1 = (X+4)^log(5) - ln(4+e^(X*log(5))).
3. Press [GRAPH]. Adjust window if needed (X from 0 to 15).
4. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 2:zero.
5. Select left bound, right bound, and guess.

desmos.instructions: Type (x+4)^{log_{10}5} - \ln(4+e^{x \log_{10}5}) and click the intersection with the x-axis.

calculator.description: Solve for x graphically

steps.title: Analyze behavior at boundaries
steps.content:
To justify the uniqueness of this solution, we examine the behavior of $F(x)$ at the boundaries.

At $x=0$:
$$\begin{aligned} F(0) &= (0+4)^k - \ln(4+e^{0}) \\ &= 4^k - \ln(5) \\ &\approx 2.636 - 1.609 \approx 1.027 > 0 \end{aligned}$$

As $x \to \infty$, the term $\ln(4+e^{kx}) \approx \ln(e^{kx}) = kx$. The term $(x+4)^k$ grows much slower than $kx$ (since $k < 1$ and polynomial growth is slower than linear for large $x$ in this context, or simply comparing powers). Thus, the negative linear term dominates, and:
$$F(x) \to -\infty \text{ as } x \to \infty$$

Since $F(x)$ starts positive and goes to $-\infty$, it must cross the x-axis at least once.

steps.title: Justify uniqueness using the derivative
steps.content:
To prove there is *exactly* one solution, we examine the derivative $F'(x)$ to determine the shape of the curve.

$$F'(x) = k(x+4)^{k-1} - \frac{k e^{kx}}{4+e^{kx}}$$

Let's analyze the sign:
1. The first term $k(x+4)^{k-1}$ is positive and decreasing (since $k-1 < 0$).
2. The second term $\frac{k e^{kx}}{4+e^{kx}}$ is positive and increasing (approaching $k$).

Since we subtract an increasing term from a decreasing term, $F'(x)$ is strictly decreasing. This means $F(x)$ is **concave down** or has a single maximum (it goes up then down).

Since $F(0) > 0$ and $F(x) \to -\infty$, and the function only changes direction once (from increasing to decreasing), it can only cross the x-axis exactly once in the decreasing portion of the curve.

Therefore, the solution $x \approx 8.203$ is unique.

highlights.text: Exactly one solution by the same monotonicity argument for \(F'\)

steps.title: Find the value of k
steps.content:
First, we use the given condition $$(f\circ g)(6)=5$$ to determine the value of $k$. Computing the composite function:

$$\begin{aligned} (f\circ g)(x) &= f(g(x)) \\ &= f(\ln(x+4)) \\ &= e^{k\ln(x+4)} \\ &= e^{\ln((x+4)^k)} \\ &= (x+4)^k \end{aligned}$$

Substitute $x=6$ and set the result to 5:

$$(6+4)^k = 5 \implies 10^k = 5$$

Solving for $k$:

$$k = \log_{10}(5) \approx 0.699$$

gdc.instructions: Calculate log10(5)

ti84.instructions: Calculate log(5) (base 10) to find k.

desmos.instructions: Type log(5) or log_10(5)

calculator.description: Calculate the value of k

steps.title: Set up the equation
steps.content:
We are asked to solve $$(x+4)^k=\ln(4+e^{kx})$$. We will use the **Graphical Intersection** method. Define two functions based on the left and right sides of the equation using our value for $k$:

$$y_1 = (x+4)^{\log_{10}5}$$
$$y_2 = \ln(4+e^{x\log_{10}5})$$

steps.title: Graph and find intersection
steps.content:
Enter both functions into your graphing calculator and view them in the window where $x \ge 0$. Use the intersection tool to find the coordinate where the curves cross.

The calculator will show an intersection at approximately $$x \approx 8.203$$

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter y1 = (x+4)^log10(5)
3. Enter y2 = ln(4+e^(x*log10(5)))
4. Draw graph
5. Use G-Solve > ISCT (Intersection)

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = (X+4)^log(5)
3. Enter Y2 = ln(4+e^(X*log(5)))
4. Press [GRAPH] (Adjust window if needed, e.g., Xmin=0, Xmax=15)
5. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) > 5:intersect
6. Select First curve, Second curve, and Guess

desmos.instructions:
1. Define k = log10(5)
2. Type y = (x+4)^k
3. Type y = ln(4+e^(kx))
4. Click the point where the lines cross

calculator.description: Graph the functions and find the intersection

steps.title: Justify uniqueness
steps.content:
To justify that the solution is unique, we observe the shape of the graphs:

1.  **LHS ($y_1$):** Since $0 < k < 1$ (specifically $k \approx 0.699$), the function $y_1=(x+4)^k$ is increasing but concave down (its rate of growth slows down).
2.  **RHS ($y_2$):** The function $y_2=\ln(4+e^{kx})$ behaves like $\ln(e^{kx}) = kx$ for large $x$, which is linear. It is concave up.

At $x=0$, $y_1(0) = 4^k \approx 2.63$ and $y_2(0) = \ln(5) \approx 1.61$, so $y_1 > y_2$. As $x \to \infty$, the linear growth of $y_2$ will eventually exceed the sub-linear growth of $y_1$. Since one curve is concave down and the other concave up (or nearly linear), they can only cross exactly once.

steps.title: State the final answer
steps.content:
The graphs intersect at exactly one point.

$$x = 8.203$$

parts.content: Find \((f\circ g)^{-1}(y)\) with domain/range.
parts.markscheme:
- Set \(y=(x+4)^k\) and rearrange: \(x=y^{1/k}-4\) <marks>M1 A1</marks>
- State inverse: \((f\circ g)^{-1}(y)=y^{1/k}-4\) <marks>A1</marks>
- Domain \((0,\infty)\), range \((-4,\infty)\) <marks>A1</marks>

4 marks total

steps.title: Form the composite function
steps.content:
To find the inverse of the composite function, we first need to find an expression for the composite function itself, $(f \circ g)(x)$.

This means we substitute the function $g(x)$ as the input into $f(x)$:

$$ (f \circ g)(x) = f(g(x)) = f(\ln(x+4)) $$

steps.title: Simplify using log laws
steps.content:
Now we substitute this input into the expression for $f(x) = e^{kx}$.

$$ (f \circ g)(x) = e^{k \ln(x+4)} $$

We can simplify this using the laws of logarithms:
1. Power rule: $a \ln b = \ln(b^a)$
2. Inverse property: $e^{\ln u} = u$

Applying the power rule first:
$$ e^{\ln((x+4)^k)} $$

Then applying the inverse property:
$$ (f \circ g)(x) = (x+4)^k $$

gdc.instructions: Type e^(2*ln(3)) and press EXE

ti84.instructions: Type e^(2*ln(3)) and press ENTER

desmos.instructions: Type e^(2*ln(3))

calculator.description: Verify the log power rule numerically (e.g., e^(2*ln(3)) should equal 3^2 = 9)

steps.title: Rearrange to invert
steps.content:
To find the inverse function, we set the simplified composite function equal to $y$ and rearrange the equation to make $x$ the subject.

$$ y = (x+4)^k $$

Raise both sides to the power of $\frac{1}{k}$ to eliminate the exponent on the right side:

$$ y^{\frac{1}{k}} = x + 4 $$

Subtract 4 from both sides:

$$ x = y^{\frac{1}{k}} - 4 $$

steps.title: State the inverse function
steps.content:
Since the question asks for $(f \circ g)^{-1}(y)$ (a function in terms of $y$), our rearranged equation is the final answer.

$$ (f \circ g)^{-1}(y) = y^{\frac{1}{k}} - 4 $$

steps.title: Determine domain and range
steps.content:
Finally, we determine the domain and range of the inverse function.

**Range of inverse:**
This is equal to the **domain** of the original composite function $(f \circ g)(x)$.
The question states $x > -4$, so the range is $(-4, \infty)$.

**Domain of inverse:**
This is equal to the **range** of the original composite function $(f \circ g)(x) = (x+4)^k$.
Since $x > -4$, we have $(x+4) > 0$. With $k > 0$, the value $(x+4)^k$ can be any positive real number.
So the domain is $(0, \infty)$.

**Final Answer:**
- Function: $(f \circ g)^{-1}(y) = y^{\frac{1}{k}} - 4$
- Domain: $y > 0$ or $(0, \infty)$
- Range: $(f \circ g)^{-1}(y) > -4$ or $(-4, \infty)$

steps.title: Method Strategy
steps.content:
To find the inverse of the composite function $(f \circ g)^{-1}(y)$, we will use the **Inverse Composition Rule**. This rule states that the inverse of a composite function is the composition of the inverses in reverse order:

$$(f \circ g)^{-1}(y) = (g^{-1} \circ f^{-1})(y) = g^{-1}(f^{-1}(y))$$

This means we need to find the inverse of $f$ and the inverse of $g$ separately, and then substitute $f^{-1}(y)$ into $g^{-1}$.

steps.title: Find the inverse of f
steps.content:
First, let's find the inverse of $f(x) = e^{kx}$. We start by setting $y = f(x)$ and solving for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= e^{kx} \\ \ln(y) &= kx \\ x &= \frac{\ln(y)}{k} \end{aligned}$$

Thus, the inverse function is:
$$f^{-1}(y) = \frac{\ln(y)}{k}$$

steps.title: Find the inverse of g
steps.content:
Next, we find the inverse of $g(x) = \ln(x+4)$. Again, we set $y = g(x)$ and solve for $x$:

$$\begin{aligned} y &= \ln(x+4) \\ e^y &= x+4 \\ x &= e^y - 4 \end{aligned}$$

So, the inverse function is:
$$g^{-1}(y) = e^y - 4$$

steps.title: Apply composition rule
steps.content:
Now we apply the rule $(f \circ g)^{-1}(y) = g^{-1}(f^{-1}(y))$. We substitute our result for $f^{-1}(y)$ into the function $g^{-1}$:

$$\begin{aligned} (f \circ g)^{-1}(y) &= g^{-1}\left( \frac{\ln(y)}{k} \right) \\ &= e^{\left( \frac{\ln(y)}{k} \right)} - 4 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Simplify the expression
steps.content:
We can simplify the exponential term using the laws of logarithms ($a \ln b = \ln(b^a)$) and the property that $e^{\ln x} = x$:

$$\begin{aligned} e^{\frac{\ln(y)}{k}} &= e^{\frac{1}{k}\ln(y)} \\ &= e^{\ln(y^{1/k})} \\ &= y^{1/k} \end{aligned}$$

Substituting this back into our expression gives the final inverse function:

$$(f \circ g)^{-1}(y) = y^{1/k} - 4$$

steps.title: Determine Domain and Range
steps.content:
Finally, we determine the domain and range of the inverse function. Recall that:
*   The **domain** of the inverse is the **range** of the original function $(f \circ g)$.
*   The **range** of the inverse is the **domain** of the original function $(f \circ g)$.

**Finding the Domain:**
The range of $g(x)=\ln(x+4)$ is all real numbers. Since $f(u)=e^{ku}$ maps all real numbers to $(0, \infty)$, the range of the composite function is $(0, \infty)$.
Therefore, the **domain** of the inverse is **$(0, \infty)$**.

**Finding the Range:**
The domain of the original function is given by the restriction on $g(x)$, which is $x > -4$. 
Therefore, the **range** of the inverse is **$(-4, \infty)$**.

parts.content: Write down the equation of the function in vertex form.
parts.markscheme:
- Substitute $(h,k)=(1,-3)$ into vertex form $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$ <marks>M1</marks>

- State equation $f(x) = a(x-1)^2 - 3$ <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>The value of $a$ is not specified in the question, so it remains as a parameter</note>

steps.title: Recall the vertex form
steps.content:
We use the vertex form of a quadratic equation. This form is particularly useful when the vertex coordinates are known:

$$f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$$

where $(h, k)$ represents the vertex of the parabola and $a$ represents the vertical stretch factor.

steps.title: Identify vertex coordinates
steps.content:
From the question, we identify the coordinates of the vertex $(h, k)$.

$$\begin{aligned} h &= 1 \\ k &= -3 \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Substitute into the formula
steps.content:
Substitute the values $h = 1$ and $k = -3$ directly into the vertex form equation:

$$\begin{aligned} f(x) &= a(x - 1)^2 + (-3) \\ f(x) &= a(x - 1)^2 - 3 \end{aligned}$$

The question notes that the graph "opens upwards", which implies that the parameter $a$ is positive ($a > 0$). However, since no other point is provided to calculate the exact value of $a$, we leave it as a parameter in the final equation.

steps.title: Verify with a graphing calculator
steps.content: You can verify that this form produces a vertex at $(1, -3)$ by graphing the function with a sample value for $a$ (e.g., $a=1$).

gdc.instructions:
1. Go to Graph menu
2. Enter y1 = (x - 1)^2 - 3
3. Draw the graph
4. Use G-Solve (F5) > MIN (F3) to find the vertex

ti84.instructions:
1. Press [Y=]
2. Enter Y1 = (X - 1)^2 - 3
3. Press [GRAPH]
4. Press [2nd] [TRACE] (CALC) and select 3:minimum
5. Set Left Bound, Right Bound, and Guess

desmos.instructions:
1. Type f(x) = (x - 1)^2 - 3
2. Click on the parabola's vertex to reveal its coordinates

calculator.description: Graph the function to verify the vertex location

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The equation of the function in vertex form is:

$$f(x) = a(x-1)^2 - 3$$

steps.title: Identify the parent function
steps.content:
This problem involves finding the equation of a quadratic by transforming the parent function. This relates to **Topic 2: Functions** (specifically SL 2.11—Transformation of functions).

Start with the simplest quadratic function, the parent graph, which has its vertex at the origin $(0,0)$:

$$y = x^2$$

steps.title: Apply horizontal translation
steps.content:
The question states the new vertex is at an $x$-coordinate of $1$. To move the vertex from $x=0$ to $x=1$, we apply a horizontal translation of 1 unit to the right.

In function notation, a shift to the right by $h$ units corresponds to replacing $x$ with $(x-h)$.

$$y = (x - 1)^2$$

steps.title: Apply vertical translation
steps.content:
The vertex has a $y$-coordinate of $-3$. To move the vertex from $y=0$ to $y=-3$, we apply a vertical translation of 3 units downwards.

In function notation, a shift down by $k$ units corresponds to subtracting $k$ from the function.

$$y = (x - 1)^2 - 3$$

steps.title: Include the vertical scale factor
steps.content:
The graph "opens upwards", but we do not know its specific steepness (width). The parent graph $y=x^2$ has a vertical scale factor of 1, but generally, any quadratic can be vertically stretched or compressed by a factor $a$.

We include the parameter $a$ to represent this. Since it opens upwards, we know $a > 0$.

$$f(x) = a(x - 1)^2 - 3$$

steps.title: State the final equation
steps.content:
The equation in vertex form $f(x) = a(x-h)^2 + k$ is determined by the vertex $(h,k) = (1, -3)$ and the scale factor $a$.

$$f(x) = a(x - 1)^2 - 3$$

gdc.instructions: Graph y = (x-1)^2 - 3. Use Analysis > G-Solve > Min to confirm the vertex is at (1, -3).

ti84.instructions: Enter Y1 = (X-1)^2 - 3. Press 2nd + TRACE (CALC) > minimum. Check if the minimum is at (1, -3).

desmos.instructions: Type f(x) = a(x-1)^2 - 3. Add a slider for 'a'. Notice that for any 'a', the vertex remains at (1, -3).

parts.content: Determine the domain and range of the function.
parts.markscheme:
- State domain: $x 
\in \mathbb{R}$ <marks>A1</marks>

- Identify minimum value is $-3$ because graph opens upwards with vertex at $(1,-3)$ <marks>R1</marks>

- State range: $y \geq -3$ <marks>A1</marks>

3 marks total

<note>Must use correct mathematical notation for both domain and range</note>

steps.title: Determine the domain
steps.content:
First, we consider the definition of a quadratic function. A quadratic function is a polynomial of degree 2. 

Polynomial functions are defined for all real numbers because there are no restrictions like division by zero or square roots of negative numbers. Therefore, the domain is the set of all real numbers.

$$x \in \mathbb{R}$$

steps.title: Analyze vertex for range
steps.content:
To find the range, we analyze the properties given in the question. We are told the graph **opens upwards** and has a **vertex at $(1, -3)$**.

Since the parabola opens upwards, the vertex represents the minimum point of the function. This means the $y$-coordinate of the vertex is the lowest possible value the function can take.

steps.title: Visualize with calculator
steps.content:
We can verify this behavior by graphing a representative function. Using the vertex form $y = a(x-h)^2 + k$ with $a=1$ (since it opens upwards), $h=1$, and $k=-3$, we get $y = (x-1)^2 - 3$.

Graphing this confirms that the parabola goes down to $y=-3$ and then goes up towards infinity.

gdc.instructions: Go to Graph menu. Enter y = (x-1)^2 - 3. Draw the graph.

calculator.description: Graph the function to visualize domain and range

steps.title: State the range
steps.content:
Since the minimum value is $-3$ and the graph extends upwards infinitely, the function can take any value greater than or equal to $-3$. 

We write this analytically as:

$$y \geq -3$$

steps.title: Final Answer
steps.content:
The domain is all real numbers, and the range is all real numbers greater than or equal to $-3$.

**Domain:** $$x \in \mathbb{R}$$
**Range:** $$y \geq -3$$

steps.title: Visualize the graph
steps.content:
To solve this problem using graphical visualization, we first sketch the parabola based on the information given. 

We are told the graph has a **vertex** at $(1, -3)$ and **opens upwards**. 

1.  Draw a coordinate plane.
2.  Plot the vertex point $(1, -3)$. This is the turning point of the graph.
3.  Since it opens upwards, draw a curve starting from the vertex and extending up and out towards the left and right.

Your sketch should look like a "U" shape with its lowest point at $y = -3$.

steps.title: Determine the domain
steps.content:
The **domain** represents all possible $x$-values for which the function is defined. 

Look at your sketch horizontally (left to right). Because the parabola widens as it goes up, it covers every possible $x$-value from negative infinity to positive infinity. There are no vertical asymptotes or holes.

Therefore, the domain is the set of all real numbers:

$$x \in \mathbb{R}$$

steps.title: Determine the range
steps.content:
The **range** represents all possible $y$-values (outputs) of the function. 

Look at your sketch vertically (bottom to top). 
- The lowest point on the graph is the vertex, which has a $y$-coordinate of $-3$.
- The graph extends upwards from this point towards positive infinity.
- The graph does not exist below $y = -3$.

This means the function can take any value greater than or equal to $-3$:

$$y \geq -3$$

steps.title: Verify with a calculator
steps.content: You can visualize this on your graphing calculator by plotting a sample function that fits the description, such as $y = (x-1)^2 - 3$. This is the vertex form $y = a(x-h)^2 + k$ with $a=1$, $h=1$, and $k=-3$.

Based on our graphical analysis, we can state the domain and range using correct mathematical notation.

**Domain:**

$$y \geq -3$$

questions.specification:
Lucas owns a chocolate factory. The amount of chocolate, in kilograms, Lucas sells in one week, $Q$, is given by $Q = 882 - 45p$ where $p$ is the price of a kilogram of chocolate in euros (EUR).

Lucas earns $(p - 6.80)$ EUR for each kilogram of chocolate sold.

To calculate his weekly profit $W$, in EUR, Lucas multiplies the amount of chocolate he sells by the amount he earns per kilogram.

labeledSubSubTopics.title: Question Type 3: Finding points of intersection of two more difficult functions
labeledSubSubTopics.reasoning: Part (c) forms profit as W=Q(p)·(p−6.80), i.e., multiplying a linear demand function by a linear earning-per-unit term to create W(p) (a quadratic function in p).

parts.content: Write down how many kilograms of chocolate Lucas sells in one week if the price of a kilogram of chocolate is $8$ EUR.

highlights.text: price of a kilogram of chocolate is $8$ EUR

steps.title: Substitute the value
steps.content:
Substitute $p = 8$ directly into the equation for $Q$:

$$Q = 882 - 45(8)$$

steps.title: Perform calculation
steps.content:
First, multiply $45$ by $8$:

$$45 \times 8 = 360$$

Then subtract this from $882$:

$$Q = 882 - 360 = 522$$

steps.content:
Lucas sells $522$ kilograms of chocolate.

According to the markscheme, finding the value $522$ earns the mark.

parts.content: Find how much Lucas earns in one week, from selling chocolate, if the price of a kilogram of chocolate is $8$ EUR.
parts.markscheme:
- Attempt to substitute values into the profit formula: $522(8 - 6.80)$ <marks>M1</marks>
- 626 (EUR) or 626.40 <marks>A1</marks>

2 marks total

<note>Award <marks>M1</marks> for multiplying the answer to part 1 by $(8 - 6.80)$. Follow through from part 1.</note>

steps.title: Analyze the profit calculation
steps.content:
The question explains that to find the weekly profit $W$, we need to multiply the quantity of chocolate sold by the earnings per kilogram. 

$$W = \text{Quantity} \times \text{Earnings per kg}$$

highlights.text: multiplies the amount of chocolate he sells by the amount he earns per kilogram

steps.title: Calculate the quantity (Q)
steps.content:
First, calculate the quantity of chocolate sold, $Q$, when the price $p$ is $8$ EUR. Substitute $p = 8$ into the given equation:

$$\begin{aligned} Q &= 882 - 45(8) \\ Q &= 882 - 360 \\ Q &= 522 \text{ kg} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate earnings per kilogram
steps.content:
Next, calculate how much Lucas earns for each kilogram sold at this price. Substitute $p = 8$ into the earnings expression:

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Earnings per kg} &= p - 6.80 \\ &= 8 - 6.80 \\ &= 1.20 \text{ EUR} \end{aligned}$$

steps.title: Calculate total weekly profit
steps.content:
Finally, multiply the quantity ($522$) by the earnings per kilogram ($1.20$) to find the total weekly profit $W$:

$$\begin{aligned} W &= 522 \times 1.20 \\ W &= 626.40 \text{ EUR} \end{aligned}$$

Lucas earns $626.40$ EUR in one week.

This matches the markscheme requirement to substitute values and find the final profit.

steps.title: Form the profit equation
steps.content:
The weekly profit $W$ is calculated by multiplying the quantity of chocolate sold ($Q$) by the earnings per kilogram. We are given the expression for $Q$ and the earnings per kilogram.

$$\begin{aligned} \text{Quantity } Q &= 882 - 45p \\ \text{Earnings per kg} &= p - 6.80 \end{aligned}$$

By substituting these into the definition of profit, we get a single equation for $W$ in terms of $p$:

$$W = (882 - 45p)(p - 6.80)$$

steps.title: Substitute the price
steps.content:
The question asks for the profit when the price of a kilogram of chocolate is $8$ EUR. Substitute $p = 8$ directly into the profit equation:

$$W = (882 - 45(8))(8 - 6.80)$$

highlights.text: price of a kilogram of chocolate is $8$ EUR

steps.title: Evaluate the expression
steps.content:
Simplify the terms inside the parentheses first. 

For the quantity term:
$$882 - 45(8) = 882 - 360 = 522$$

For the earnings term:
$$8 - 6.80 = 1.20$$

Now, multiply these values to find the total weekly profit:
$$W = 522 \times 1.20 = 626.40$$
